design_philosophy.core_loop: Flip tiles to propel enemy dolls into walls or blocks, destroying them all to clear each level. Tiles hide patterns underneath and reveal new traversal options. Single-screen puzzle-action with spatial reasoning.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Doll collision avoidance vs. active tile manipulation. Players must anticipate enemy movement patterns while managing limited tile options and darkness mechanics in later levels.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Rescue girlfriend from kidnapper by navigating surreal palace worlds, using environmental manipulation (tile flipping) rather than direct combat. Cooperative multiplayer rescues add social fantasy.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately old-school arcade sensibility: single-screen puzzle rooms, no scrolling, simple controls on complex spatial problems. Rejects modern action-adventure conventions in favor of distilled mechanic mastery. Game Freak's debut shows design philosophy centered on 'beautiful simplicity' with decorative, colorful aesthetic that Hudson Soft had to defang for Western markets (reducing cuteness for commercial viability).

difficulty.lives_system: Separate life tracking per player in co-op. One player losing all lives forces solo continuation for remaining player until both exhaust lives.
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-level checkpointing only. No mid-level checkpoints. Boss battles end each palace world (10 levels + boss per world).
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Debug menu allows invincibility toggle and custom starting lives count, indicating developer expected varied skill levels. Darkness rooms force tile anticipation rather than reactive play, creating execution difficulty variance.

progression.upgrade_system: Stars collected during play (tracked separately per player) and tile pattern reveals. Patterns hidden under flipped tiles grant gameplay advantages; graffiti-locked tiles and bolted metal tiles create stalemate win conditions.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Palace world sequence fixed. Level select hidden behind debug code (Game Genie). Boss gates each palace; defeating all dolls progresses to next palace. Scene sequences show girlfriend being transported between palace locations.
replayability_hooks: Separate star tracking encourages high-score competition
Cooperative multiplayer with asymmetric life management
Hidden level select via debug code rewards exploration
Tile pattern variations under repeated flips create discovery moments
Darkness levels require strategic pre-planning, increasing replay learning curve

world_building_techniques: Scene sequences between palace worlds (girlfriend kidnapping plot advancement)
Enemy doll thematic variation per palace (artist dolls draw pictures, swimmers swim)
Japanese version: narrative context (younger sister Quinty's jealousy) vs. American version (girlfriend trapped in dream)
Visual world theming through doll types and environmental tiles
Regional censorship of suggestive imagery (nude torso replaced with school uniform in US) reflects cultural narrative layers

notable_bosses: Each palace ends with boss encounter (specific mechanics not documented in provided sources)

faction_indicators: Kidnapper faction: younger sister Quinty (JP) or unnamed girl (US) represents antagonist force
Rescue faction: player character (Bon-Bon/Carton) and girlfriend (Candy/Jenny) form protagonist bond
danger_signals: Enemy doll collision = instant life loss
Darkness rooms reduce visual information, increasing environmental danger perception
Bolted tiles and graffiti-locked tiles signal unbreakable environmental hazards
Boss gates at palace ends escalate difficulty narrative
environmental_storytelling: Five-second timer tile (kanji '伍'/five in US version provides mechanical context hint)
Artist dolls creating pictures signal creative palace (House of Vinci)
Spinner tiles enable aggressive enemy repositioning, signaling advanced puzzle mechanics
Palace-to-palace progression shows kidnapper's domain expansion (multiple palaces holding girlfriend)
Tile pattern reveals under repeated flips suggest hidden depth/secrets in world structure

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Mendel Palace (tcrf.net)
